coredata.dc:title: In-situ green synthesis of copper tannic acid framework in the presence of graphene quantum dots: Improved colloidal and antibacterial properties
coredata.dc:creator: Azizi, Jahanghir
Javanbakht, Siamak
Mohammadi, Reza
coredata.dc:description: This work reports the preparation of a copper tannic acid MOF (CuTA) and graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanocomposite, GQD/CuTA, by a simple, environmentally friendly, and inexpensive method with exceptionally enhanced antibacterial properties. To end this, GQDs were first fabricated from citric acid using a neoteric, simple and straightforward hydrolysis approach and followed by they effectively anchor MOF nanoparticles (CuTA) by a green hydrothermal in situ synthesizing method. The constructed GQD/CuTA nanomaterials were characterized and validated using XRD, FT-IR, TEM, DLS, UV–vis, and PL techniques. Significant antibacterial activity against E. coli bacteria for both CuTA and GQD/CuTA (0 CFU/mL) and S. aureus bacteria for CuTA and GQD/CuTA (300 and 40 CFU/mL) was observed for the GQD/CuTA nanocomposite. The MTT assay showed good cytocompatibility for the GQD/CuTA nanocomposite against human dermal fibroblast cells (HFF-2). The result suggests that the synthesized GQD/CuTA nanocomposite with improved colloidal and antibacterial properties has the potential to be used as a safe photoluminescent nanoplatform with strong antimicrobial activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antibacterial
Metal-organic framework
Green chemistry
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the University of Tabriz .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis of GQD/CuTA nanocomposite.
FTIR spectra of synthesized (a) CuTA MOF, (b) GQDs, and (c) GQD/CuTA nanocomposite.
XRD patterns of synthesized (a) CuTA MOF, and (b) GQD/CuTA nanocomposite.
FESEM images of (a) CuTA, and (b) GQD/CuTA samples. EDX spectra of (c) CuTA, and (d) GQD/CuTA samples. Elemental mapping of (e-g) CuTA, and (h-j) GQD/CuTA samples.
Particle size distribution of (a) CuTA MOF, and (b) GQD/CuTA nanocomposite. Colloidal stability of CuTA MOF and GQD/CuTA nanocomposite after dispersion (c) and one week (d).
UV–vis absorption spectra of synthesized CuTA MOF, GQDs, and GQD/CuTA nanocomposite.
PL spectra of (a) GQDs, and (b) GQD/CuTA nanocomposite under excitation at 365 nm.
Release profile of TA from GQD/CuTA nanocomposite at pH 7.4.

Digital images of viable colonies of S. aureus and E. coli on agar plates for control (a, b, a', b'), CuTA (c, d), GQD/CuTA (e, f), GQD/Cu2+ (g, h), and GQD/TA (i, j) samples after 24 h of incubation.
Cell viability of HFF-2 cells treated with different concentrations of GQD and GQD/CuTA samples after 48 h of incubation. The values are reported as mean ± SD of three experiments.
Statistical antibacterial activity of different samples against S. aureus and E. coli after 24 h of incubation.

row.entry: Average number of S. aureus bacteria (CFU/mL)
Average number of E. coli bacteria (CFU/mL)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: The values are reported as mean ± SD of three experiments.

head.ce:title: In-situ green synthesis of copper tannic acid framework in the presence of graphene quantum dots: Improved colloidal and antibacterial properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Research Laboratory, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Polymer Research Laboratory, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports the preparation of a copper tannic acid MOF (CuTA) and graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanocomposite, GQD/CuTA, by a simple, environmentally friendly, and inexpensive method with exceptionally enhanced antibacterial properties. To end this, GQDs were first fabricated from citric acid using a neoteric, simple and straightforward hydrolysis approach and followed by they effectively anchor MOF nanoparticles (CuTA) by a green hydrothermal in situ synthesizing method. The constructed GQD/CuTA nanomaterials were characterized and validated using XRD, FT-IR, TEM, DLS, UV–vis, and PL techniques. Significant antibacterial activity against E. coli bacteria for both CuTA and GQD/CuTA (0 CFU/mL) and S. aureus bacteria for CuTA and GQD/CuTA (300 and 40 CFU/mL) was observed for the GQD/CuTA nanocomposite. The MTT assay showed good cytocompatibility for the GQD/CuTA nanocomposite against human dermal fibroblast cells (HFF-2). The result suggests that the synthesized GQD/CuTA nanocomposite with improved colloidal and antibacterial properties has the potential to be used as a safe photoluminescent nanoplatform with strong antimicrobial activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of disease-causing microorganisms like bacteria, fungi, and viruses is the main reason for morbidity and mortality (Costerton et al., 1999; Wei et al., 2019). These microorganisms can be found in different parts of human life, such as air, water sources, food production, and medical facilities which can pose a serious risk to human health (Alavijeh et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2020). The infection caused by harmful bacteria is a notable danger for global public health, leading to many deaths every year. In order to treatbacterial infections, many powerful antibiotics with broad coverage have been utilized in high amounts because of the high rates of mortality and morbidity caused by these bacteria. However, this has led to an increase in antibiotic resistance, making it difficult to find effective treatments (Courtney et al., 2016; Que et al., 2005; Shima et al., 2016). Therefore, it is essential to develop antibiotic alternatives or antibiotic delivery systems for deactivating bacteria with drug resistance (Allahverdiyev et al., 2011; Smith, 2005).

In the past few years, extensive research has been conducted to investigate novel antibacterial materials for the treatment ofbacterial infections, including cationic polymers (Liu et al., 2020a), nanozymes (Chen et al., 2018; Han et al., 2018), and metal oxide nanoparticles (Chai et al., 2020). Although these materials have been effective in recent years, some of them still have limitations such as low efficiency and environmental problems (Ingle et al., 2014; Ma et al., 2017; Sirelkhatim et al., 2015). Consequently, it is necessary to develop antibacterial materials that are highly effective and offer several advantages, like high efficiency, great biocompatibility, prolonged efficacy, low dosage requirement, and low environmental pollution (Li et al., 2021b). In this regard, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have been created as exceptional nanoplatforms for biomedical purposes (Liu et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2020b; Pan et al., 2020).

MOFs are porous inorganic–organic hybrid crystalline materials with low density, made up of metal nodes (metal ions or clusters) and organic linkers (bipodal or multipodal organic ligands) (Furukawa et al., 2013; James, 2003). They are considered to be highly promising fortherapeutic applications due to the regulation and release of antibacterial agents through various mechanisms.These mechanisms include releasing antibacterial ligands (Au-Duong and Lee, 2017), metal ions like Ag, Cu, and Zn (Sava Gallis et al., 2019), active species that are trapped within the MOF (Chowdhuri et al., 2017), or antibacterial agents from modified MOF structure (Esfahanian et al., 2019).

Key components in the design ofantibacterial MOFsare metal ions andcoordination ligands which contribute to the overall performance and functionality of the MOFs. Metal ions are essential for the formation and properties of MOFs. (Yaghi et al., 2003). There are numerous metal ions that exhibitantibacterial characteristics and can be employed in preparing antibacterial MOFs, including Ag, Cu, Zn, Cd, and Hg. These metal ions exhibit intrinsicantimicrobial propertiesand have been used as antimicrobial agents for environments (Lemire et al., 2013; Pulido and Parrish, 2003; Yan et al., 2022). In addition to metal ions, the choice of coordination ligands is also essential in the antibacterial activity of MOFs. Coordination ligands are responsible for connecting metal ions and forming the framework structure of MOFs (Kitagawa et al., 2004). Various organic ligands, such as imidazolates, triazolates, and carboxylates, have been used for the synthesis of antibacterial MOFs (Carné-Sánchez et al., 2013). Tannic acid (TA) is a type of polyphenolic compound and has significant antibacterial properties due to its ability to disrupt bacterial cell walls (Quideau et al., 2011). Additionally, when it is combined with antibacterial medications, it can improve their effectiveness in killing bacteria (Akiyama et al., 2001; Chung et al., 1998). Utilizing TA as MOF ligand has opened up new opportunities for developing antibacterial materials. TA-based MOF exhibit enhanced antibacterial properties compared to their individual components (Zhuang et al., 2017). This synergistic effect is mainly due to the release of TA and metal ions from the MOF structure. These components interact with bacterial cell walls and disrupts their integrity, leading to cell death (Li et al., 2019).

Recently, modification of MOFs with carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs) has emerged as a promising strategy to enhance their properties and expand their applications (Ahmed et al., 2022). CQDs are a type of carbon-based nanomaterials with unique optical and electronic properties, such as high photostability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility (Ahmed et al., 2022; Dong et al., 2022). The combination of MOFs and CQDs can lead to the formation of MOF-CQD composites with improved properties, such as enhanced photocatalytic activity, improved sensing performance, and increased stability (Ajibade and Oloyede, 2022). Therefore, the modification of MOFs with CQDs represents a novel avenue for the creation of advanced materials with enhanced potential applications.

Herein, a nanocomposite of copper tannic acid MOF (CuTA) and graphene quantum dot (GQD), GQD/CuTA, was developed through a simple, green and cost-effective method with exceptional improved antibacterial properties. To end this, a simple bottom-up thermal pyrolysis approach was used to synthesize GQDs from citric acid as commercially available starting material. Then, in-situ synthesizing method was utilized to grow CuTA in the presence of GQDs toward the synthesis of GQD/CuTA nanocomposite (Fig. 1 ). TA contains numerousphenolic hydroxyl groups, which contribute to the material's ability to disperse and exhibit strong antibacterial properties. The presence ofcopper ionschelated to the TA further enhances the ability of MOF to combatbacterial strains. On the other hand, owing to the aqueous solubility of GQDs, the prepared photoluminescent GQD/CuTA showed a great colloidal stability which could enhance the antibacterial activity against gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria.
Citric acid, TA, NaOH and all other chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. and used without further purification.

CuTA MOF was prepared through an oxidative coordination reaction of Cu2+ with TA (Li et al., 2021a). In this regard, a homogenous solution was prepared by dissolving 0.250 g of TA and 0.825 g of CuSO4·5H2O in 50 mL of deionized water. Then, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.4 by adding NaOH (2 M) solution, gradually. The mixture was heated to 50 °C and kept for 4 h. The resulting product was separated by centrifugation and washed with deionized water and ethanol three times.

GQDs were synthesized by a simple and green bottom-up thermal pyrolysis approach reported in the literature (Dong et al., 2012). In order to prepare GQDs in atypical procedure, a 5 mL beaker containing 1 g of citric acid was heated to 200 °C using a heating mantle for 35 min. After 35 min, the color of the liquid was changed from pale yellow to orange, indicating the creation of GQDs. Then, the acquired orange liquid from GQD preparation, was slowly added to the 50 mL of 5 mg/mL NaOH solution, with vigorouslystirring the mixture. The GQD-containing solution was neutralized with NaOH until it reached a pH of 7.0, leading to the formation of anaqueous GQD solution.

0.05 g of TA was added to 100 mL of GQD aqueous solutionand its pH was approximately adjusted to 5–6 by HCl solution. Then, 1.75 g of CuSO4·5H2O was added to the mixture and stirred to be completely dissolved. Afterward, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.4 by adding NaOH (2 M) solution, gradually. The mixture was heated to 50 °C and kept for 5 h. The resulting product was separated by centrifugation and washed with deionized water and ethanol three times.

The morphology of synthesized CuTA MOF and GQD/CuTA nanocomposite was observed using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, TESCAN, MIRA3, Czech Republic) coupled with an energy-dispersive X-ray analyzer (EDX, TESCAN, Czech Republic, model SAMX). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Bruker Instruments, model Aquinox 55, Germany) was used to obtain IR spectrums in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Tongda, TD-3700, China) patterns were obtained by a diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation in the range of 10-70°. UV–visible spectroscopywas performed using a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 35 UV–visible absorption spectrometer. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed with a DLSZP/Particle Sizer (Microtrac, model Nanotrac Wave). The photoluminescence (PL) properties of synthesized GQD and GQD/CuTA nanocomposite was determined using a spectrofluorometer (LS45, PerkinElmer).

In order to assess thein vitrorelease profile of GQD/CuTA nanocomposite, 20 mg of GQD/CuTA was added to 10 mL of phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.4) as a release medium to simulate the physiological environment. The solution was then kept in a shaker incubator at 37 °C. At specified intervals of time, 2 mL of the solution was sampled, and the fresh phosphate buffer solution with the same volume was replaced. The sampled solutions in different time intervals were characterized by UV–visspectrophotometer at 282 nm, and the concentration of released TA in each sample was determined by its prepared calibration curve. Finally, the released drug concentration was calculated by Eq. (1).
(1)
C n
=
C
n . s
+
V s
V t
∑
s = 1
n - 1
C s
In this equation, n is the number of withdrawal steps of the releasing medium, Cn is the concentration of released drug (ppm), Cn.s is the concentration obtained from calibration curve (ppm), Cs is the concentration of previous sample obtained from calibration curve (ppm), Vs is the volume of sample (2 mL) and Vt is the total volume of release solution (10 mL).

The colony forming unit (CFU) method was used to investigate the antibacterial activity of the synthesized CuTA MOF and GQD/CuTA nanocomposite against Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) bacteria. First, CuTA MOF and GQD/CuTA nanocomposite were ultrasonically dispersed in sterile water. Then, 1 mL of each sample was introduced to 1 mL of bacterial solution with a concentration of 105 CFU/mL. The solution was treated at 37 °C for 2 h in an incubator. After incubation, 100 μL of the solution was taken out, diluted serially in PBS solution, and spread on nutrient agar plates. All of the agar plates were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Eventually, the number of viable colonies was counted. Moreover, to compare the individual anti-bacterial efficacy of Cu2+ and TA with GQD, GQD/Cu2+ and GQD/TA samples were also prepared and their anti-bacterial activity was investigated according to the above method. The bacterial solution without the addition of synthesized samples was also regarded as the control group.

MTT test was used to assess thein vitrocytotoxicity of synthesized GQD and GQD/CuTA nanocomposite against HFF-2 cell lines. HFF-2 cells were seeded in 96-well plates with a density of 104 cells in each well and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, the cells were treated with 5 different concentrations (8–128 µg/mL) of each sample and incubated at 37 °C for 48 h under the atmosphere of 5% carbon dioxide gas. After that, the medium of the cells was removed, and the wells were filled with 20 μL of MTT solution with a concentration of 5 mg/mL. The plate was then incubated at 37 °C for 4 h at dark. After draining the MTT solution, 150 μL of DMSO was added to each well and shaken in order to dissolve the formed formazan crystals. Finally, the optical density of each well was measured at 570 nm by a microplate reader, and the cell viability was calculated using Eq. (2). Each concentration of the samples had three replicates, and HFF-2 cells without any treatment were regarded as the control group.
(2)
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
( % )
=
M e a n
o p t i c a l
d e n s i t y
o f
e a c h
s a m p l e
M e a n
o p t i c a l
d e n s i t y
o f
c o n t r o l
g r o u p
× 100

The FTIR spectra of thesynthesized sampleswere examined thoroughly to verify the synthesis of CuTA MOF, GQDs, and GQD/CuTA nanocomposite (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Fig. 2a, the FTIR spectrum of CuTA showed a narrow absorption peak at 3390 cm−1 which is attributed to COH vibration of TA, implying the coordination of TA with copper ions by the phenolic groups. Furthermore, the infrared activation vibration mode of CuO was identified by the absorption peaks at 482 cm−1 and 601 cm−1, further confirming the formation of CuTA chelate system (Chen et al., 2003; Zou et al., 2006). For the synthesized GQD, a wide absorption peak around 3400 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH groups. Moreover, the stretching vibration of COH groups can be seen at around 1080 cm−1. Two absorptions peaks at 1571 cm−1 and 1406 cm−1 are the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COO– groups, respectively. Hence, the existence of these characteristic peaks confirms the successful synthesis of GQDs (Dong et al., 2012). The FTIR spectrum of GQD/CuTA displays both the characteristic absorption bands related to CuTA and GQDs. Therefore, these findings can indicate the successful modification of coordinated CuTA MOF with GQDs and the formation of GQD/CuTA nanocomposite.

XRD analysis was employed to analyze the crystalline structures of CuTA MOF and GQD/CuTA nanocomposite and the acquired XRD patterns are shown in Fig. 3 . The characteristic peaks of CuTA were detected at 2θ 22.80°, 35.64°, and 52.60°, indicating a crystalline structure consistent with the XRD pattern of CuTA in previous reports (Li et al., 2021a). Furthermore, the characteristic peak of Cu was detected at 2θ 16.60°, confirming the presence of copper ions in the structure (Yin et al., 2022). After modification of CuTA with GQD, the typical peaks of CuTA were disappeared and a wide peak was detected in the XRD pattern of GQD/CuTA at 2θ 26.96°, verifying the presence of graphene structures (Javanbakht and Namazi, 2017). The disappearance of sharp peaks in the XRD pattern of GQD/CuTA indicates that the synthesized GQD/CuTA has an amorphous structure. Thus, the variation in the crystalline structure of CuTA after modification can confirm the successful synthesis of GQD/CuTA nanocomposite.

In order to investigate the effect of GQDs on the structure of synthesized nanocomposite, morphological studies were performed. Fig. 4 illustrates the FESEM images of CuTA and GQD/CuTA in different magnifications. According to Fig. 4a, CuTA MOF has randomly distributed ultrathin rice grain-like structures with an average width of 35 nm. Moreover, the elemental mapping confirmed the presence of Cu, O, and C elements in the CuTA structure, indicating the coordination of copper ions with TA and formation of a MOF. The synthesis of CuTA in the presence of GQDs significantly changed its morphology. Spherical uniformly distributed nanoparticles with an average size of 30 nm can be seen in the structure of GQD/CuTA (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, according to elemental mapping analysis, Cu and O elements are distributed uniformly with strong intensity on the surface, proving the successful synthesis of GQD/CuTA nanocomposite.

The hydrodynamic size of the synthesized materials was measured using theDLS analysis. According to Fig. 5 a-b, formation of CuTA MOF in the presence of GQDs decreased the average particle size from 921 nm for CuTA to 77 nm for GQD/CuTA. The reduction in particle size can be due to the nanoscale nature of the synthesized GQDs and great colloidal solubility (stable even after one week, Fig. 5c), making the formation of CuTA in a smaller size.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of CuTA, GQD, and GQD/CuTA were investigated to determine their absorption bands wavelength and optical properties. As shown in Fig. 6 , CuTA has a main peak at 247 nm, which is due to the coordinated TA molecules (Hao et al., 2018). On the other hand, GQD shows an absorption peak at 365 nm, attributed to surface functional CO groups, n-π* transition (Dong et al., 2012). The absorption spectrum of GQD/CuTA nanocomposite was approximately similar to CuTA with lower intensity at 243 nm. This could be due to the coordination of GQDs with Cu2+ ions and accordingly TA molecules through hydrogen bonding (Sinduja and John, 2016), which reduces peak intensity.

The optical characteristics of GQD and GQD/CuTA were further characterized by PL study. Fig. 7 a displays the PL spectrumof GQDs under excitation at 365 nm wavelength. Theemission peakat around460 nm, which is a blue emission, was observed, revealing the strong PL activity of GQDs. This is due to abundant sp2 structures and oxygen-containing functional groups in the structure of GQD, affecting its bandgap and energy levels, leading to the emission of light (Dong et al., 2012; Sun et al., 2013). For GQD/CuTA, two shoulder emission peaks at 440 nm and 490 nm, were observed; probably, attributed to the recombination of electron-hole pairs in theGQDandCuTA (Fig. 7b). Thus, incorporation of GQDs in CuTA can trigger the PL properties in nanocomposite.

To clarify the capability of the synthesized GQD/CuTA nanocomposite as antibacterial agent delivery system, thein vitrorelease profile of TA was investigated under simulated physiological conditions (pH = 7.4, 37 °C) and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . The results exhibited a time-dependent release manner, and the release of TA increased with time. GQD/CuTA showed a considerable intense release in the first hour, which is necessary for some medicinal applications. Although the TA release rate decreases after the first hour, it still has a sustainable amount, indicating the long-term effectiveness of the TA. The reason is that the surface layers of the nanocomposite release the coordinated TA rapidly, causing an intense release at early times. However, by decreasing the concentration of the TA in surface layers, it is released from the inner layers of the nanocomposite, and the release rate is decreased.

Cytotoxicity assay of GQDs and GQD/CuTA nanocomposite was investigated via cell viability in order to investigate the cytocompatibility of synthesized systems. In this regard, HFF2 cell lines were selected as they are of primitive human origin, which can show more effective physical reactions to bacterial agents than other cells. To end this, HFF-2 cell lines were treated with different concentrations of GQD and GQD/CuTA samples. As can be seen in Fig. 10 , GQD exhibited low cytotoxicity against HFF-2 cells after 48 h of incubation, in which the cell viability was over 75% in all studied concentrations. Whereas, GQD/CuTA nanocomposite showed higher cytotoxicity in different concentrations, compared to GQD. This happens due to the presence of copper ions and TA with antimicrobial properties in the structure of nanocomposite, which can attack the walls of cells and destroy them. The above findings indicate that the cytotoxicity of GQD and GQD/CuTA samples are dose-dependent and cell viability decreases by increasing treatment concentration. Therefore, it is essential to find the optimum treatment concentration with the best efficiency and biocompatibility. It is indicated that the cell viability remined over about 75% at 32 µg/mL concentration of GQD/CuTA nanocomposite after 48 h of incubation.

In the current study, an environmentally friendly CuTA MOF was synthesized with antibacterial properties through a coordination reaction between copper ions and TA. In addition, a simple and cost-effective bottom-up approach was used to prepare GQDs. In-situ synthesis of CuTA MOF in the presence of GQDs resulted in the development of GQD/CuTA nanocomposite with improved colloidal and antibacterial properties. Investigation on the morphological properties and size distribution of nanocomposite revealed the formation of uniform nanoscale structures in the presence of GQDs. The successful synthesis and incorporation of GQDs in CuTA MOF were approved by different techniques (i.e., FTIR, XRD, DLS, UV–vis and SEM). Furthermore, GQD/CuTA nanocomposite had PL activity, indicating its optical properties. The CFU method exhibits that the prepared nanocomposite showed an excellent antibacterial activity against S. aureus and E. coli strains. Accordingly, the synthesized nanocomposite could attract great attention for construction of safe photoluminescent systems. In addition, this work provides an efficient way to construct improved antibacterial GQD/CuTA nanocomposite, which could be a promising system for potential applications in multi-resistant antibacterial agents.

Jahanghir Azizi: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Project administration, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Siamak Javanbakht: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Reza Mohammadi: Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the University of Tabriz.

ce:section.ce:para: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author, upon reasonable request.
